name: McGee Bend Dam
short_description: Rockhound the shoreline of Sam Rayburn Reservoir near McGee Bend Dam for quartz, jasper, and agate specimens. Flat terrain and easy access make this an excellent beginner-friendly East Texas collec...
description:
McGee Bend Dam impounds Sam Rayburn Reservoir in southeast Texas, creating a productive rockhounding location along the shoreline and exposed banks. The reservoir sits within the East Texas Piney Woods, an area shaped by Miocene to Pliocene sedimentary deposits and the uplift of the Sabine Uplift. Erosion of surrounding Tertiary sediments and older Cretaceous formations has concentrated quartz pebbles, jasper fragments, and chalcedony specimens along the water line, particularly after water level fluctuations expose fresh material.

This location offers one of the most accessible rockhounding opportunities in the Piney Woods region. The flat terrain and proximity to the dam make it suitable for families and beginning collectors. The best finds typically appear along the shoreline during periods of lower water levels, where exposed banks reveal colorful jasper and well-rounded quartz specimens.

Collectors should focus on the exposed clay and sandy banks near the dam's base and along coves where wave action concentrates heavier minerals. The calm waters of the reservoir mean collecting is generally safe, though attention to water level changes and weather is important. Spring flooding can dramatically alter the landscape and expose new material.

Access is generally straightforward from Sam Rayburn town, though specific areas may vary in public availability. Always check current conditions and respect any posted restrictions near the dam structure itself.
history: The Sabine Uplift and surrounding East Texas sedimentary formations were uplifted and eroded during the Quaternary, concentrating resistant quartz and jasper minerals along river courses and in depositional basins. Sam Rayburn Reservoir, impounded in 1966, created new erosional patterns that expose Tertiary-age quartz-rich sediments and weathered jasper deposits along its shoreline.
tips: Search the shoreline directly along the water line and in small coves where wave action accumulates heavier material - colorful jasper pieces and translucent quartz pebbles concentrate here. Focus on exposed clay banks after seasonal water level drops, as freshly eroded material is most accessible. Bring a classifier or small screen to sift sandy material near the base of banks; many specimens are pea to marble-sized. Early morning visits during lower water periods yield the best finds before sun and erosion scatter loose material.
terrain_notes: Elevation 140 feet. Flat terrain with gentle slope to water. Shoreline is sandy and silty with exposed clay banks along coves. Vegetation includes mixed pine and hardwood typical of East Texas. Walking distance from parking is minimal to moderate (typically under 0.5 miles). Footing is generally stable on compacted soil and sand, but banks can be slippery when wet.
best_season: March - May, September - November. Spring (March-May) brings lower water levels that expose fresh material on banks. Fall (September-November) offers cooler temperatures ideal for outdoor collecting. Summer heat and high water levels in June-August reduce accessible exposed material.
written_directions: From Sam Rayburn, take TX-21 east toward the dam. Follow signs for Sam Rayburn Reservoir and McGee Bend Dam access roads. Approach the dam from the south side where public access areas near the spillway typically allow shoreline rockhounding. Parking areas are located near the dam visitor facilities. GPS coordinates 31.056845, -94.10297 mark the dam structure; approach from designated public access points only.
road_conditions: Paved roads to dam area. Access roads are maintained. Final approach to shoreline may be unpaved but passable with standard vehicles.
parking_notes: Parking available near the dam visitor area and access points. Multiple pullouts along access roads. Space is generally adequate on weekdays; weekends may see higher traffic.
collection_rules: Personal collection only. Respect posted signs and dam facility boundaries. No collection directly on or immediately adjacent to the dam structure itself. Check with local ranger station or dam office for any temporary restrictions. No motorized excavation or large-scale removal.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Respect posted boundaries near dam
No excavation or disturbance of banks
Check for temporary restrictions before collecting
No commercial use
nearest_services: Sam Rayburn town, approximately 3 miles northwest. Gas, food, and supplies available in Sam Rayburn and nearby Center, Texas.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most ability levels. Parking close to shoreline access. No wheelchair-specific facilities noted, but flat ground makes walking manageable for most people. Water access is direct from parking areas with minimal elevation change. Sturdy footwear recommended due to uneven sandy/clay shoreline.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at McGee Bend Dam in Texas?
faq.answer: The primary finds at McGee Bend Dam are quartz specimens, jasper in various colors, agate fragments, and chert. Quartz pebbles are abundant along the shoreline, while colorful jasper pieces accumulate in coves where wave action concentrates heavier material. Most specimens are pea to marble-sized, making them ideal for collectors of all skill levels.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at McGee Bend Dam near Sam Rayburn?
faq.answer: No, there is no fee for rockhounding at McGee Bend Dam. It is open to the public for personal collection at no charge. However, respect posted boundaries and do not collect directly on or near the dam structure itself. Always check with the dam office or local rangers for any temporary restrictions before visiting.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound McGee Bend Dam and Sam Rayburn Reservoir?
faq.answer: The best seasons are March through May (spring) and September through November (fall). Spring brings lower water levels that expose fresh material on banks, while fall offers cooler temperatures. Summer water levels are typically higher and conceal much of the shoreline material. Winter is also reasonable but less predictable due to weather.

faq.question: How do you access McGee Bend Dam for rockhounding, and what is the terrain like?
faq.answer: Access is straightforward from Sam Rayburn via TX-21 heading toward the dam. The area is flat with gentle slopes to the water, making it beginner-friendly. Shoreline terrain is sandy and silty with exposed clay banks. Walking distances from parking to productive collecting areas are typically under 0.5 miles, though footing can be slippery when wet.

faq.question: Is McGee Bend Dam a good rockhounding spot for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, McGee Bend Dam is an excellent beginner and family location. The flat terrain, easy access, and abundance of small specimens make it perfect for first-time collectors. Children ages 6 and up can participate, and dogs are welcome. No permits or special skills are required - just bring a collecting bag and start searching the shoreline.

faq.question: What tools and techniques work best for collecting at McGee Bend Dam?
faq.answer: Bring a small screen or classifier to sift sandy material near the base of banks where many specimens hide. Focus your search directly along the water line and in small coves where wave action accumulates material. Early morning visits during lower water periods yield the best finds. Wear sturdy footwear due to uneven shoreline terrain, and bring water and sun protection for comfortable outdoor collecting.

meta_description: Find quartz, jasper, and agate at McGee Bend Dam near Sam Rayburn, Texas. Free public access. Beginner-friendly flat terrain.
